posts.name: Fred C
posts.cooked:
Took a flyer on a 1976 Nicolas Clos St. Denis.

Any information on the maker? CT shows a “Nicolas” Clos St. Denis with vintages dating back to 1929.

posts.name: Ian Sutton
posts.cooked: Nicolas presumably the French wine merchant of the same name. Pretty awful shops these days, but presumably a lot better back in the day.

posts.name: Thomas Keim
posts.cooked:
Nicolas was a huge negociant back in the day. I have had quite a bit of their older Burgundy bottlings, and they can be great bargains. They used to be from some pretty reliable sources back then. The last bottlings I’ve had from them were a couple of '72s in decent but not great shape provenance wise - and they were still in fine shape. Their old wines always had a kind of “clumsy” overtone but were usually pretty full bodied and forward -

'76 was a terrific Burgundy vintage, if the price is right, I would jump on it -

posts.name: Fred C
posts.cooked: Ian and Thomas, thanks for the info. It’s always fun to get a little history behind the bottles.

posts.name: W E R D N A - S K R O B A C K
posts.cooked:
Thomas Keim:

'76 was a terrific Burgundy vintage, if the price is right, I would jump on it -

I always heard the acidity was problematic. No?
posts.display_username: W E R D N A - S K R O B A C K

posts.name: Gerhard P.  (P r @ € s e n t)
posts.cooked:
A S K R O B A C K:
Thomas Keim:

'76 was a terrific Burgundy vintage, if the price is right, I would jump on it -

I always heard the acidity was problematic. No?

Many 76 have softened now … I had some fine bottles during the last years … and they were initially more tannic than acidic …
Nicolas - the French merchant - did fine bottlings in the 60ies, 70ies - it seems he had good sources, a CV 76 was excellent 1.5 years ago.
posts.display_username: Gerhard P.  (P r @ € s e n t)

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
A S K R O B A C K:
Thomas Keim:

'76 was a terrific Burgundy vintage, if the price is right, I would jump on it -

I always heard the acidity was problematic. No?

1976 was an extremely hot vintage with very thick skins, so tannin rather than acidity was the issue here. I suspect the reason it’s comparatively easy to find '76s these days is that they shut down hard and people put them in a corner of the cellar and forgot about them. I had a nice 1er cru Beaune recently that was still vibrant and firm at the core. It is not on the level 1971 or 1978, however, at least in my limited experience.

posts.name: Brady Daniels
posts.cooked:
William Kelley:
A S K R O B A C K:
Thomas Keim:

'76 was a terrific Burgundy vintage, if the price is right, I would jump on it -

I always heard the acidity was problematic. No?

1976 was an extremely hot vintage with very thick skins, so tannin rather than acidity was the issue here. I suspect the reason it’s comparatively easy to find '76s these days is that they shut down hard and people put them in a corner of the cellar and forgot about them. I had a nice 1er cru Beaune recently that was still vibrant and firm at the core. It is not on the level 1971 or 1978, however, at least in my limited experience.

I agree with William. The 76’s I’ve had have been distinctively sweet, sometimes candied, and obviously from a warm vintage. That aspect really reminds me of 2003 burgs. (I know it should be the other way around, but I’ve had more '03’s.)
'76 is my birth year…is it fair to assume that GC’s are still going to be enjoyable? I’ve got a 40th to plan for next year.

posts.name: Brady Daniels
posts.cooked:
Kirk.Grant:

'76 is my birth year…is it fair to assume that GC’s are still going to be enjoyable? I’ve got a 40th to plan for next year.

If the provenance is good, your odds are good.

posts.name: Fred C
posts.cooked:
Gerhard P.:

Many 76 have softened now … I had some fine bottles during the last years … and they were initially more tannic than acidic …
Nicolas - the French merchant - did fine bottlings in the 60ies, 70ies - it seems he had good sources, a CV 76 was excellent 1.5 years ago.

This is very interesting as both of the 76s we tried still showed prominent tannins and we knew nothing about the vintage character.

The Nicolas CSD softened up and became quite nice. The Bonneau du Martray Corton was a tannic bomb from start to finish.

posts.name: Paul Jaouen
posts.cooked:
Ian Sutton:

Nicolas presumably the French wine merchant of the same name. Pretty awful shops these days, but presumably a lot better back in the day.

I think there are 38 wine shops in Beaune now and this is one of them. I don’t know how they stay open and who would shop there.
